text: The harbour office at Port Aldwick was rebuilt in 1947 after a fire destroyed the original timber structure. The new building is made of local grey granite and has three floors. The harbourmaster's records, kept on the top floor, list 412 registered fishing vessels in 1950, falling to 87 by 1995. The office still operates today and is open to the public on weekday mornings.

Marrow Lake is a freshwater lake covering roughly 4.2 square kilometres in the eastern uplands. It is fed by two streams, the Tor and the Welk, and drains southward into the Aldwick river. The lake freezes over in most winters but rarely for more than three weeks. A single island near its northern shore holds the ruins of a 14th-century chapel.

The Pellman Bridge carries the county road across the Aldwick gorge. It was completed in 1908, spans 96 metres, and rises 41 metres above the water at its centre. The bridge was strengthened in 1972 to carry heavier traffic and was closed to vehicles over seven tonnes after an inspection in 2011. Pedestrians may still cross at all times.

Dr Helena Marsh founded the Aldwick Botanical Institute in 1961 with a grant from the county council. The institute maintains a herbarium of about 60,000 pressed specimens and a living collection of 900 plant species. Marsh served as director until 1984 and published four monographs on upland mosses. The institute remains independent of any university.

The Welk Mill produced woollen cloth from 1820 until it closed in 1958. At its peak in the 1880s it employed around 340 workers and ran on water power from the Welk stream, later supplemented by a coal-fired steam engine installed in 1891. The mill building was converted into apartments in 2004; its original waterwheel is preserved in the courtyard.

Aldwick County holds a market every Thursday in the central square, a tradition recorded since at least 1389. The market charter was granted by royal decree and renewed several times. Today around 50 stalls trade on a typical Thursday, selling produce, textiles, and household goods. A smaller farmers' market was added on the first Saturday of each month in 1998.

The Tor Observatory sits at 720 metres on Pellman Hill and was built in 1932. It houses a 0.5-metre reflecting telescope, replaced once in 1989. The observatory is run by a volunteer astronomical society with about 120 members and opens to the public on clear Friday evenings between October and March. Light pollution from the valley has increased since 2000, limiting faint-object viewing.

Aldwick railway station opened in 1864 on the line between Port Aldwick and the county town. The original station had two platforms and a goods yard; the goods yard closed in 1965. Passenger services were reduced to a single line in 1970. The station building, a listed structure, was restored in 2015 and now also houses a café and a small local-history exhibition.
